elements.sent-2411-001: However, this critical feature is at risk when the transport link relies on wireless technology.
elements.sent-2411-002: Fortunately, solutions exist to enhance the reliability of wireless links.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper, we recall the theoretical fronthaul model, present a dynamic reconfiguration strategy and perform a conclusive experiment.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, we showcase the setup of a wireless fronthaul testbed and discuss the resulting measurements.
elements.sent-2411-005: For this task, we leveraged the commercial hardware provided by the \gls{hfcl , a private 5G network with \gls{mmwave radio access interface.}}
elements.sent-2411-006: Our experiments provide original data on the fronthaul utilization in this real deployment, demonstrating both a good accordance with the theoretical model discussed in \cite{shapingWirelessFH and the viability of one stabilizing solution.. \color{black.}}
elements.sent-2411-007: Operators and the industry at large are recognizing the value of decentralization, driving advancements that offer significant benefits in network flexibility and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-008: Modern Ethernet-based fronthaul interfaces, such as \gls{ecpri and \gls{oran Open Fronthaul, facilitate this flexibility, allowing operators to rethink their transport networks and introduce heterogeneity.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: This capability to reimagine transport networks is remarkably helpful in optimizing network performance and cost.
elements.sent-2411-010: The newly introduced flexibility enables operators to explore transport network alternatives to dedicated fibers\cite{wfh_survey1 , including E-band radio links, extremely high-frequency point-to-multipoint radio links, and even free-space optics.}
elements.sent-2411-011: These alternatives present a promising reduction in capital and operational expenditures, especially in areas where new fiber deployment is prohibitively expensive.
elements.sent-2411-012: The wireless-vs-wired technology comparison in \cite{wfh_cost_effectivenes shows that, in appropriate conditions, wireless technologies can provide sufficient link capacity at a cost even lower than wired counterparts.}
elements.sent-2411-013: However, despite these cost benefits, alternative wireless approaches often need to improve reliability compared to dedicated fiber connections.
elements.sent-2411-014: Due to adverse atmospheric conditions, high-frequency fronthaul links and \acrlong{fso are susceptible to link quality degradation.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Additionally, packet-switched fronthaul networks are vulnerable to congestion, further complicating their deployment.
elements.sent-2411-016: Traditionally, the reliability issue in wireless fronthaul links has been mitigated by over-dimensioning, a strategy that is inefficient and costly, negating many of the positive aspects of alternative solutions.
elements.sent-2411-017: Adaptive solutions present a more efficient and cost-effective approach.
elements.sent-2411-018: Previous work by the authors has introduced a dynamic and adaptive mechanism that manages instantaneous access resources to accommodate transitory fronthaul capacity reductions caused by effects such as rain \cite{shapingWirelessFH .}
elements.sent-2411-019: This network comprises a macro 5G \gls{gnb operating at \gls*{mmwave , covering Politecnico di Milano campus and the surrounding neighborhood.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: The base station is dedicated solely to research and experimentation, ensuring high experimental repeatability.
elements.sent-2411-021: The high capacity of \gls*{mmwave let us test the fronthaul connection under extreme access loads.}
elements.sent-2411-022: The deployed hardware and tailored setup facilitate testing across a broad range of controlled conditions.
elements.sent-2411-023: This setup positions \gls{hfcl as one of the few facilities worldwide capable of precisely replicating typical adverse atmospheric effects on advanced \gls*{mmwave 5G networks.}}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this work, we revisit the theoretical foundations of fronthaul rate adaptation mechanisms and describe two experiments applying these principles in a real network environment.
elements.sent-2411-025: Specifically, the first experiment demonstrates the feasibility of the Cell Reconfiguration strategy, while the second validates the theoretical fronthaul rate model presented in \cite{shapingWirelessFH .}
elements.sent-2411-026: This study aims to provide the first insights into the real-world performance of fronthaul systems in front of varying channel conditions that lead to capacity reduction, potentially contributing to more effective wireless \gls{cran deployments.}
elements.sent-2411-027: The paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-028: The next section provides context on \gls{cran fronthaul and section~\ref{sec:backgroundSplits discussed the available literature on this theme.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Section~\ref{sec:model reviews theoretical fronthaul rate model and adaptation strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-030: The experimental section follows, detailing the testbed setup in Section~\ref{sec:HFCL and the experiments in Section~\ref{sec:numerical .}}
elements.section-2411-002: Background on C-RAN and functional splits
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.97\linewidth]{images/phy_chain_rev3.jpg.png} \caption{Physical layer functions chain and eCPRI splits \cite{eCPRI_standard}} \label{fig:phy_chain}
elements.sent-2411-032: Part of this chain, namely the physical layer functions, are reported in Figure~\ref{fig:phy_chain , for both \gls{ul and \gls{dl directions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Not all the functions are implemented in the same physical device, but they are split between two separate entities, connected via a fronthaul link.
elements.sent-2411-034: Depending on the reference architecture, these two entities have been named either \gls{aau and \gls{bbu , or \gls{ru and \gls{du .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: The decisio where to implement specific functions is known as split option.
elements.sent-2411-036: Among the possible splits, the \gls{ecpri standard defines five options -- three for \gls{dl and two for \gls{ul -- shown in Figure~\ref{fig:phy_chain .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-037: For instance, if the network configuration chooses split option $I_D$, all functions from the RF front-end to the QAM modulation are implemented in the \gls{aau , while higher-layer functions are handled in the \gls{bbu .}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Recently, \gls{oran Alliance has introduced additional split options, which expand upon the eCPRI-defined options \cite{oran_splits .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Depending on the chosen split, different types of data are transmitted over the fronthaul.
elements.sent-2411-040: For example, basic bits are transmitted at split option $I_D$, while IQ symbols -- specifically bit-encoded IQ symbols -- are found at option $II_D$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Generally, the fronthaul data rate (or fronthaul rate) increases as the split moves from higher to lower layers in the chain, eventually saturating at split E.
elements.sent-2411-042: At this level, all unused \gls{rb are zero-padded, and the rate, reaching its peak, becomes constant.}
elements.sent-2411-043: This behavior contrasts with splits above E, where the fronthaul rate depends on the data requested by users.
elements.sent-2411-044: This principle underpins the model presented in \cite{shapingWirelessFH and partially reviewed in Section~\ref{sec:model .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: While latency and coordination requirements also vary across split options, this paper wants to analyze the feasibility of adaptation techniques for wireless fronthaul, therefore these aspects fall outside the scope of this paper.
elements.sent-2411-046: In this context, understanding how to model fronthaul link capacity -- and the extent to which real implementations align with these models -- is crucial.. \color{black.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In this section, we address both the broader context of our research -- covering the theoretical foundations of \gls{cran splits and \gls{ran reconfiguration strategies -- and the availability of testbeds for the practical evaluation of the fronthaul aspects, which is our primary focus.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Various studies have investigated the requirements of \gls{cran splits.}
elements.sent-2411-049: For instance, \cite{survey_functional_split_2019 offers a comprehensive review of different functional splits, ranging from low-PHY to PDCP/RRC, highlighting their pros and cons along with formulas for estimating fronthaul rate requirements.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Additionally, \cite{darts presents simulation data to illustrate typical fronthaul rate needs.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Building on these foundational works, \cite{shapingWirelessFH introduces a more general and updated model, providing new insights into fronthaul rate dimensioning.}
elements.sent-2411-052: While these references offer critical theoretical context, they do not explore practical implementations.
elements.sent-2411-053: Research on RAN reconfiguration underscores the potential to adjust \gls{cran splits and resources to meet various requirements, such as power, latency, and fronthaul capacity.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The concept of Joint Access and Fronthaul Coordination has been examined as both a static optimization problem \cite{matoussi , and a time-varying one \cite{alba, fh-aware_optimization, quantizationResolution .}}
elements.sent-2411-055: While we acknowledge and share some foundational ideas from this research, the introduction of flexible splits complicates the reconfiguration process significantly.
elements.sent-2411-056: In contrast, our approach focuses specifically on modifying MIMO layers and resource blocks, offering a simpler strategy that can be readily implemented in commercial systems.
elements.sent-2411-058: Notable contributions include \cite{testbed_mountaser and \cite{testbed_salama , which focus on fiber-based fronthaul deployments.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: The work in \cite{testbed_mountaser presents an OAI-based testbed to evaluate fronthaul rate, latency, and jitter, while \cite{testbed_salama measures data rate and processing power for various splits.}}
elements.sent-2411-060: The most recent study, \cite{challenges_opportunities_wfh , combines modeling with experiments on a fixed-capacity wireless fronthaul link.}
elements.sent-2411-061: While these studies offer valuable insights, our work distinguishes itself by focusing on a fronthaul testbed constructed with commercial hardware.
elements.sent-2411-062: This emphasis on practical evaluation addresses a gap in the literature, enhancing the understanding of fronthaul performance in commercial 5G deployments through a comprehensive set of measurements.
elements.sent-2411-063: In summary, while previous literature has established the theoretical and conceptual groundwork, this paper advances the state of the art by providing a comprehensive analysis of fronthaul performance on a testbed utilizing commercial equipment.
elements.sent-2411-064: This work helps bridge the gap between theory and practical application in CRAN architecture.. \color{black.}
elements.sent-2411-065: To provide theoretical foundations to the management strategies that are the focus of the experiments, this section discusses a fronthaul rate model.
elements.sent-2411-066: This model derives from \cite{shapingWirelessFH and only reports the formulas for split $I_D$ and $I_U$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: The rates that the fronthaul link must support are: \begin{equation}R_{FH}^{I_D} = N_{RB} \cdot N_{SC} \cdot N_{MIMO} \cdot Q_M \cdot\frac{1}{T_S}, \label{R_I_D}\end{equation} \begin{equation}R_{FH}^{I_U} = N_{RB} \cdot N_{SC} \cdot N_{MIMO} \cdot N_{IQ} \cdot\frac{1}{T_S}, \label{R_I_U}\end{equation} for the \gls{dl and the \gls{ul , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Both formulas consider: $N_{RB}$, the number of used \glspl{rb by the radio access interface, $N_{SC}$, the number of subcarriers per \gls{rb (i.e., twelve), $N_{MIMO}$, the number of concurrently active \gls{mimo layers, and $T_S$, the duration of an OFDM symbol.}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Two parameters differ between the two formulas above: $Q_M$, which represents the number of bits per modulated by each symbol, and $N_{IQ}$, the number of bits used to encode an IQ sample (i.e., the bitwidth).
elements.sent-2411-070: In other words, this means that the \gls{dl fronthaul carries flows of raw data bits while the \gls{ul fronthaul carries bit-encoded IQ symbols.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: Since $N_{IQ}$ is usually bigger than $Q_M$, the \gls{ul fronthaul rate is usually higher than the \gls{dl counterpart.}}
elements.sent-2411-072: It is important to note that these formulas include an approximation.
elements.sent-2411-073: Beyond the \gls{re data, the fronthaul link must also carry antenna control information, with beamforming control being particularly significant.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Further details on this aspect are available in \cite{shapingWirelessFH .}
elements.sent-2411-075: We do not consider this contribution further in the paper, as it is highly implementation-dependent.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, its impact is expected to be minor in our testbed, which uses hybrid beamforming and thereby significantly reduces the antenna control information compared to full-digital beamforming.
elements.sent-2411-077: Ensuring this constraint is typically manageable when dedicated optical fiber links are used for transport; however, it becomes a significant technical challenge when links are wireless or fiber is shared, as link capacity is no longer under the designer’s control.
elements.sent-2411-078: If the fronthaul capacity falls short of the rate requirements, the system may attempt to maintain an effective fronthaul connection by adjusting access resources, leveraging the relationship between access and fronthaul.
elements.sent-2411-079: Specific countermeasures can limit the number of \glspl{rb , $N_{RB}$, and \gls{mimo layers, $N_{MIMO}$, available to the gNB's scheduler \cite{shapingWirelessFH .}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: In particular, the cell reconfiguration can be implemented by selectively disabling certain radio access resources through software reconfiguration.
elements.sent-2411-081: This impacts the total radio access capacity according to the formula \cite{TS38.306 : \begin{equation}R_{ACC} = N_{RB} \cdot N_{SC} \cdot N_{MIMO} \cdot Q_M \cdot R_{MAX} \cdot f_{TDD} \cdot \frac{(1 - OH)}{T_S}\end{equation} where $R_{MAX}$ is the target code rate, $OH$, is the overhead due to the control channels (e.g., PBCH, PRACH), and $f_{TDD}$, is a factor dependent on the \gls{tdd frame scheme (e.g., DDDSU) activated in the network.}}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}R_{ACC} = N_{RB} \cdot N_{SC} \cdot N_{MIMO} \cdot Q_M \cdot R_{MAX} \cdot f_{TDD} \cdot \frac{(1 - OH)}{T_S}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-082: An open issue remains in evaluating the impact and effectiveness of this strategy in a real network.
elements.sent-2411-083: In the next section, we present the experimental setup, and in Section \ref{sec:numerical , we demonstrate both the viability of cell reconfiguration and the consistency of the system behavior with Eq.~\ref{R_I_D .. \color{black.}}}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.91\linewidth]{images/equipments_rev2.jpg} \caption{High-Frequency Campus Lab RAN equipment involved in the experiment} \label{fig:hfcl}
elements.sent-2411-084: All the experiments were carried out at \gls{hfcl , an open, experimental network deployed in the campus of Politecnico di Milano.}
elements.sent-2411-085: It consists of a \acrlong{sa , private 5G network equipped with tools for measuring and testing its performance.}
elements.sent-2411-086: Its deployment is the result of the joint effort of Politecnico di Milano, telecommunication companies, equipment manufacturers, and service companies, to enhance cooperation among researchers.
elements.sent-2411-087: The \gls{hfcl radio access interface operates only at \acrshort{mmwave and is only accessible from authorized \gls{cpe .}}}
elements.sent-2411-088: This allows us to produce high-fidelity 5G campaigns, excluding unwanted switching to other frequencies and interferences from external users.
elements.sent-2411-089: The full system used in the experiment is illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:hfcl .}
elements.sent-2411-090: The \gls{hfcl includes an \acrfull{aau Huawei HAAU5323, connected to a centralized \acrfull{bbu Huawei BBU5900.}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: The \gls{bbu is in turn wired to a 5G virtualized core network (not reported in the picture).}
elements.sent-2411-092: This equipment is commercially available and the underlying technology is proprietary.
elements.sent-2411-093: However, the possibility to access the \gls{gnb configuration lets us modify many of its parameters, such as the access bandwidth, the \acrfull{tdd structure and the transmitted power.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: Details on its configuration are reported in Table \ref{tab:antenna_details .}

elements.sent-2411-095: The network is completed by a \gls{cpe working as \gls{ue , namely the Huawei H312m-371.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Its main characteristics are reported in Table \ref{tab:CPE .}
elements.sent-2411-097: To collect data transfer logs (e.g., received signal power), we connect the \glspl{cpe to a PC running a drive test log tool, namely the CPE-compatible Genex Probe 8.1 software.}
elements.sent-2411-098: To carry out fronthaul experiments, the 5G network was complemented by additional devices.
elements.sent-2411-099: In order to emulate wireless links' capacity variations in our optical fiber fronthaul, we placed two Huawei RTN380AX in the middle of the fronthaul optical connection.
elements.sent-2411-100: These are variable-rate transceivers working in the E-band (71–76 or 81–86 GHz) and usually employed in backhaul point-to-point connections.
elements.sent-2411-101: Among their features, they are capable to provide large capacities for wireless and support a wide set of channel bandwidths and modulations, that allow us adjusting E-band link capacity, symmetrically in both \gls{ul and \gls{dl , with a good granularity.}}
elements.sent-2411-102: In order to better confine and control the experiment, we connected the two transceivers with a waveguide instead of a free-space link.
elements.sent-2411-103: This optimizes space and allows high reproducibility of the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-104: Finally, we included in the fronthaul chain a VIAVI MTS-5800 network tester.
elements.sent-2411-105: This device was inserted between the \gls{bbu and the waveguide to measure the bitrate crossing the fronthaul link.}
elements.sent-2411-106: We leveraged this reconfiguration capabilities in the experiments of Section~\ref{sec:numerical .}

elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{subfigure}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/dl_access_throttling_2.png} \caption{} \label{fig:access_throttling_dl} \end{subfigure} \begin{subfigure}{0.49\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{images/ul_access_throttling_2.png} \caption{} \label{fig:access_throttling_ul} \end{subfigure} \caption{Relation between fronthaul rate and access rate} \label{fig:access_throttling}
elements.sent-2411-107: This experiment aims to demonstrate the feasibility of cell reconfiguration adaptation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-108: To execute it, we leveraged two cell configurations: configuration 1 and 2 reported in Table \ref{tab:cell_config .}
elements.sent-2411-109: The methodology used was the following.
elements.sent-2411-110: We enforced a bottleneck on the fronthaul capacity by setting different configurations of the E-band transceivers.
elements.sent-2411-111: By gradually increasing this capacity, we identified the minimum amount required to support the different cell configurations.
elements.sent-2411-112: We repeated this operation for each transmission direction and for every cell configuration.
elements.sent-2411-113: The results are shown in Figure~\ref{fig:fh_throttling , where the achievable access throughput is plotted against the available fronthaul capacity.}
elements.sent-2411-114: Configuration 1 shows that the UE can reach a \gls{dl rate of 850~Mbps if the fronthaul supports at least 3.1~Gbps.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Similarly, the uplink saturates at around 200~Mbps when the fronthaul is greater or equal than 2.7~Gbps.
elements.sent-2411-116: The trends exhibit an on/off behavior: once a capacity threshold is reached, all available \glspl{rb are allocated to the UE, allowing it to achieve maximum throughput.}
elements.sent-2411-117: In configuration 2, this pattern remains but is rescaled.
elements.sent-2411-118: Configuration 2 allocates only half of the \glspl{rb , $N_{RB}$, available in configuration 1.}
elements.sent-2411-119: This causes a reduction very close to a factor two not only in the in achievable access rate but also in \gls{dl and \gls{ul required fronthaul capacities, as predicted by the Eqs.~\ref{R_I_D and \ref{R_I_U .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: The slight deviation from the exact halved value results from additional rates generated for antenna control, which are implementation-dependent.
elements.sent-2411-121: Overall, the system behavior aligns with expectations, demonstrating the viability of the cell reconfiguration strategy.
elements.sent-2411-122: To further interpret the values in the plot, we conducted the following experiment.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Analysis on the fronthaul rate requirements
elements.sent-2411-123: This experiment aims to deepen our understanding of fronthaul operations.
elements.sent-2411-124: Specifically, we show that for commonly considered fronthaul splits, the required fronthaul rate is closely linked to uplink traffic, with downlink traffic never acting as a bottleneck.
elements.sent-2411-125: For this analysis, we utilized all cell configurations listed in Table~\ref{tab:cell_config .}
elements.sent-2411-126: We employed a methodology significantly different from the previous experiment.
elements.sent-2411-127: Without imposing any limitations on fronthaul capacity -- allowing the E-band link to operate at full capacity -- we used iperf3 to generate arbitrary bitrate values between two PCs: one connected to the CPE and the other to the N6 interface of the \gls{cn .}
elements.sent-2411-128: This setup allowed us to observe how the generated bitrate translates over the fronthaul.
elements.sent-2411-129: These values are shown on the x- and y-axes in Figure~\ref{fig:access_throttling .}
elements.sent-2411-130: Note that in each plot, the traffic is actively generated only in one direction (i.e., \gls{dl in Fig.~\ref{fig:access_throttling_dl and in \gls{ul in Fig.~\ref{fig:access_throttling_ul ) yet fronthaul traffic also appears in the opposite direction.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: This is due to acknowledgments and control signals, which play a fundamental role.
elements.sent-2411-132: In Figure~\ref{fig:access_throttling_dl , the lines at the bottom of the graph indicate a linear relationship between the \gls{dl access throughput and the \gls{dl fronthaul throughput.}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: The rates for configurations 2 and 3 increase linearly from the origin, reaching a point where access rate is 460~Mbps and fronthaul rate is 680~Mbps, at which point the \gls{ue utilizes all the \glspl{rb it can support.}}
elements.sent-2411-134: Although configuration 3 supports up to 8 MIMO layers, the UE is hardware-limited to 2 layers, so all 132 \glspl{rb are allocated to the CPE, with 66 per active MIMO layer \cite{TS38.104 .}}
elements.sent-2411-135: Therefore, in the \gls{dl , configurations 2 and 3 perform similarly, each operating with a 100 MHz channel bandwidth.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Configuration 1, with a 200 MHz bandwidth, provides up to 132 \glspl{rb per MIMO layer, doubling the \gls{dl rates: \gls{dl access rate reaches 820~Mbps, and \gls{dl fronthaul rate reaches 1240~Mbps.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-137: These values closely align with those predicted by Equation \ref{R_I_D , shown as the black line in the figure.}
elements.sent-2411-138: The minor offset observed is attributed to antenna control data.
elements.sent-2411-139: The \gls{ul curves in Figure~\ref{fig:access_throttling_dl exhibit a markedly different behavior from those in the \gls{dl .}}}
elements.sent-2411-140: First, fronthaul rate values are significantly higher in the \gls{ul than in the \gls{dl , even though \gls{ul access traffic mainly consists of acknowledgment traffic.}}}
elements.sent-2411-141: This discrepancy arises from a lower \gls{ul functional split compared to the \gls{dl .}}
elements.sent-2411-142: Consequently, $N_{IQ}$-bit encoded symbols are transmitted over the \gls{ul fronthaul link rather than basic data bits, as is the case for the \gls{dl .}}
elements.sent-2411-143: It is difficult to estimate the exact value of $N_{IQ}$ as it depends on dynamic compression algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-144: Second, the \gls{ul exhibits two distinct regions: a steep rise at low access rates, driven by antenna control data, and a gradual increase at higher rates, where the impact of access resources becomes evident.}
elements.sent-2411-145: This latter behavior reflects the bits carried in transmitted OFDM symbols across the whole bandwidth during a symbol period.
elements.sent-2411-146: Importantly, only symbols with no data across all subcarriers can be omitted from transmission over the fronthaul.
elements.sent-2411-147: Partially loaded OFDM symbols must be fully transmitted on the UL fronthaul link.
elements.sent-2411-148: This explains why, at low access rates, loaded symbols are primarily driven by signaling data, whereas at higher rates, the dependence on user data becomes more pronounced.
elements.sent-2411-149: Additionally, the slope of the curve is lower than in the \gls{dl case, as new resources -- namely, additional symbols to be transferred through the fronthaul -- are only needed once the currently used symbols are fully utilized.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Analyzing the three \gls{ul curves in Figure~\ref{fig:access_throttling_dl , we observe that the values of configurations 2 and 3, which were equal in the \gls{dl , now differ by a factor of two at the same access rates.}}}
elements.sent-2411-151: At their maximum access \gls{dl rate, configuration 2 achieves 1300~Mbps in the fronthaul, while configuration 3 reaches 2630~Mbps.}
elements.sent-2411-152: TThis behavior is attributed to the number of available MIMO layers: used symbols are replicated across every antenna port and transmitted, irrespective of their activation in a specific layer.
elements.sent-2411-153: When comparing configuration 1 and configuration 3, instead, we find their fronthaul rates to be quite similar.
elements.sent-2411-154: Configuration 1 has double the bandwidth but half the MIMO layers compared to configuration 3.
elements.sent-2411-155: This aligns with Eq.~\ref{R_I_U : doubling $N_{RB}$ and halving $N_{MIMO}$ results in no change to the required fronthaul rate.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Overall, this indicates that transmitting \glspl{rb in \gls{ul necessitates sending a number of resources over the fronthaul link that is proportional to the product of total bandwidth and activated MIMO layers, accounting for allof \glspl{rb present in the same symbol period.}}}
elements.sent-2411-157: However, calculating the exact number of \glspl{rb transmitted through the fronthaul is complex, as it depends on the scheduler's allocation of bits to symbols, influencing the resulting fully unloaded symbols that can be omitted.}
elements.sent-2411-158: In Figure~\ref{fig:access_throttling_ul , an arbitrary \gls{ul throughput is enforced in the access via iperf3 .}}
elements.sent-2411-159: The trends observed previously are extended here, with a significant contribution from the \gls{ul traffic and a minor contribution from the resulting \gls{dl traffic.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: The latter occupies a negligible share of the bandwidth, while the former remains the dominant contribution.
elements.sent-2411-161: Notably, the maximum UL fronthaul occupancy remains similar to that in Figure~~\ref{fig:access_throttling_dl , despite the \gls{ul access traffic being pushed to 180 Mbps in this scenario, compared to the acknowledgment traffic generated by an 850 Mbps \gls{dl connection in the previous figure.}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: One final observation clarifies the results obtained in the cell reconfiguration experiment.
elements.sent-2411-163: Referring back to the thresholds in Figure~\ref{fig:fh_throttling , we can now conclude that these thresholds are dictated by the \gls{ul transmission, which requires approximately 2650 Mbps for an 850 Mbps DL access rate, rather than by the \gls{dl transmission, which only necessitates 1240 Mbps.}}}
elements.sent-2411-164: The experiment reported in this section demonstrates, through real measurements, the feasibility of the cell reconfiguration strategy and the overall correctness of Eqs.~\ref{R_I_D and \ref{R_I_U in describing the fronthaul resource requirements.}}
elements.sent-2411-165: While downlink fronthaul rates are easily predictable, \gls{ul fronthaul rates are significantly influenced by the implementation of the eCPRI fronthaul interface and the scheduling policy governing access traffic.}
elements.sent-2411-166: Overall, our system demonstrates that fronthaul management for downlink traffic using split $I_D$ is highly efficient, while the split $I_U$ for uplink traffic is more resource-intensive, representing the primary limitation during a reduction in fronthaul capacity.. \color{black.}
elements.sent-2411-167: In this paper, we explored the dynamic reconfiguration of 5G cells to adapt to variable fronthaul capacities in wireless links, focusing on the theoretical underpinnings of \gls{cran splits and RAN reconfiguration strategies.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Through comprehensive measurements conducted on a testbed built with commercial hardware, we provided valuable insights into fronthaul performance and its implications for real-world 5G deployments.
elements.sent-2411-169: Our findings demonstrate that the fronthaul management for downlink traffic implementing split $I_D$ is highly efficient, while the uplink split $I_U$ proves to be more resource-intensive and serves as a critical limitation under reduced fronthaul capacity conditions.
elements.sent-2411-170: The experimental results validate the accuracy of our proposed models, particularly Eqs.~\ref{R_I_D and \ref{R_I_U , in characterizing fronthaul resource requirements.}}
elements.sent-2411-171: Moreover, we established that the required fronthaul rates are predominantly influenced by uplink traffic, highlighting the need for careful consideration of scheduling policies and the implementation of eCPRI interfaces.
elements.sent-2411-172: This work not only advances the theoretical understanding of fronthaul dynamics but also bridges the gap between theory and practical applications, providing a foundation for future research in adaptive strategies for 5G networks.
